Donatello's David is one of the most famous works of the Renaissance, but we have no records of its origins: we do not know who it was made for, when, why. title: Engaging Symbols: Gender, Politics, and Public Art in Fifteenth-Century Florence by Adrian W.B. Randolph
highlights:
Gender, Politics, and Public Art in Fifteenth-Century Florence by Adrian W.B. Randolph

$74.41  ...    During the 15th century, Florence emerged as one of Europe's most important city-states. This text investigates the fascinating intersection of art, politics and gender in the public sphere of Florence at this time. Adrian Randolph identifies a pivotal moment in the history of public art when Florentines visually encoded political and social relations within gendered categories. Randolph shows how "engaging" political symbols were grounded in a revolutionary way in amorous discourses that drew on metaphors of affection, desire, courtship, betrothal, marriage, homo- and hetero-eroticism, and procreation. The author emphasizes the sculpture of Donatello and the Medici family's efforts to seek legitimacy through artistic patronage.
